topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The string quartet swelled, a vibrant tapestry of sound weaving through the ballroom. I was nursing a lukewarm champagne, my gaze sweeping the room with a practiced, almost hyper-focused scan. Every face, every movement, registered in sharp, distinct snapshots. Then, across the crowded expanse of polished mahogany, my breath hitched. There she was. Sarah. She was laughing, a sound I hadn't heard in years, her hair catching the chandelier light in a way that made my chest tighten. My hand, gripping the glass, suddenly felt clammy. I forced a slow sip of the bubbly, trying to anchor myself, to keep the sudden, jarring shift in my equilibrium from taking hold. I subtly angled my body toward the exit, a silent, urgent calculation running through my mind.

[story 2]

The air in the reception hall was thick with perfume and celebration. I kept my posture rigid, my eyes darting from the dancing couples to the distant entrance, a constant, low-level tension humming beneath my skin. It was a calculated effort to remain outwardly composed, to project an air of serene indifference. But then, the music dipped slightly, and I saw him. Mark. He was standing near the bar, talking animatedly with someone, and the sight of him—the familiar curve of his smile, the way he tilted his head—sent a distinct jolt through my system. My focus narrowed instantly. I stopped mid-sip, my fingers tightening around the stem of the glass until my knuckles were white. I took a deliberate, slow breath, letting the noise of the room wash over me, trying to filter out everything except the space between us.

[story 3]

I found myself standing near the terrace doors, pretending to examine the floral arrangements, but my attention was entirely consumed by the entrance. The noise of the celebration seemed to recede, replaced by a sudden, sharp clarity in my perception. I watched as she walked in, and the world seemed to slow down to a crawl. My heart began to thump a steady, insistent rhythm against my ribs. I consciously smoothed down my dress, a small, involuntary gesture of self-correction. When our eyes met across the expanse of the lawn, the air seemed to thin. There was a flicker of something undeniable in her expression—recognition, perhaps surprise—and I could feel a sudden, intense pressure building behind my eyes. I took a step backward, intending to retreat, but my feet felt strangely rooted to the spot.
